channel.title: Posts on Onur Celep
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Onur Celep

item.title: The rule my agent broke while reading it: evals for AI skills
item.description:
My AI setup runs on rules. Release discipline, model routing, incident playbooks — they ship to every agent session as skills: markdown instructions loaded into context, versioned in a template repo and propagated across my repositories. The setup tour covers how that works; the postmortem covers the day the CI plumbing under it failed silently, and the guardrails that came out of that.

This post is about realizing the same failure class exists one layer up — and what happened when I pointed tests at it.

item.title: The green check that did nothing: a postmortem of AI agents in CI
item.description: I run AI coding agents in CI across my repositories. The loop looks like this: I open a GitHub issue describing a change and tag the agent; it reads the repo, makes the change on a branch, and hands me a pull request. A second agent reviews every PR automatically before I look at it. A human merges everything. The setup is stamped from a single template repo (ai-factory) so all my projects run the same configuration — the full tour of that setup and how it came to be is its own post; this one is about the day it broke.

item.title: One AI setup for every repo: how I work with coding agents, and why it became a factory
item.description: I write software with AI agents in three places: a terminal session while I work, cloud sessions I fire off and forget, and agents that live in my repos’ CI. All three follow the same committed rules, read the same committed memory, and hand everything to me as a pull request that I merge. This post is a tour of that setup — what each piece is for, what actually changed in how I work, and why keeping it consistent across repositories eventually forced me to build a small platform for it (ai-factory).

item.title: Reverse-engineering the Luckfox PicoKVM into a Python client
item.description:
I do hardware-in-the-loop test automation. Part of that is driving a KVM over IP: power-cycle a board, watch the screen, send keystrokes, mount an installer image, all from a script with no human at the bench. The cheap device that does this well is the Luckfox PicoKVM. The problem: there was no Python client for it. So I wrote one and published it.

picokvm-client is a small, typed, synchronous client and CLI for the PicoKVM, on PyPI:

item.title: SSH certificate access to a device lab, end to end
item.description: I run a fleet of embedded Linux test benches. Each bench is a small gateway (a Raspberry Pi) with a board under test wired to it. People and CI jobs need to SSH into those gateways and boards to flash images, drive tests, and poke at failures. The question that looks trivial and is not: who is allowed to SSH where, and how do you keep that true over time?
